text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic myomectomy on a large 15 cm pedunculated myoma. The retroperitoneal space is opened between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament to access the external iliac vein. The umbilical artery is identified and followed posteriorly and medially to locate the uterine artery. The uterine artery is clearly visible, with its pulsation observable, and the superficial uterine vein is seen below it. The pararectal fossa is developed to ensure the ureter is safely away from the uterine artery before placing a clip at the artery's origin. The same procedure is planned for the opposite side.

The surgeon tracks the infundibulopelvic ligament medially and uses scissors to coagulate the peritoneum between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament. Care is taken to avoid damaging the external iliac vein and any varicosities. After deepening into the retroperitoneal space, small bleeders are coagulated for a clear view. The internal iliac artery is visible, and the space between the ureter and uterine artery is developed. The assistant retracts the uterine artery externally, revealing the bifurcation between the umbilical and uterine artery. The uterus and uterine artery are clearly seen, allowing for the placement of a clip on the uterine artery.

The surgeon uses bipolar coagulation and section energy to control and remove the large pedunculated myoma, ensuring a bloodless procedure due to prior control of the uterine arteries. The myomectomy is nearing completion.

The surgeon performs hemostasis on the myoma bed using a 14mm vial stitch for intracorporeal suturing. Assistants hold the edges of the uterine scar, and the surgeon takes a substantial amount of myometrial tissue to ensure effective hemostasis. A double flat knot technique is used, starting from the medial part of the scar, with two or three knots providing sufficient hemostasis. No bipolar energy is used to preserve healthy tissue for future pregnancies.

The surgeon places two additional stitches for hemostasis, targeting the anterior and posterior parts of the uterine scar. The needle is inserted into the posterior part of the scar, and the myometrium is invaginated into the myoma bed to reduce the risk of intramyometrial hematoma. A flat knot is tied securely to ensure effective hemostasis.

The surgeon completes the hemostasis around the myometrial scar, visible ischemia surrounding the area. The final stitch is placed, avoiding the proximal part of the fallopian tube. Assistants invaginate the tissue, ensuring the tube remains visible. The final suturing aspect shows controlled bleeding without the use of bipolar energy.

The surgeon inserts the morcellator into the left iliac fossa and begins morcellation of the large myoma placed in the pelvis. The setup is at seven hundred volts per minute. After morcellation, the abdominal cavity and pelvis are cleaned, ensuring no remnants of the myoma remain. Both ureters are checked, and the hole used for the morcellator is closed to reduce the risk of incisional hernia. The final view shows a bloodless scar with no bleeding or myoma pieces in the pelvis.

The surgeon demonstrates the placement of a surgical clip, showing the rotation of the ureter and the anatomical positioning of the superior vesicle artery anterior to the uterine artery. The ureter is visible with good rotation as the surgeon controls the left side.
